mnemonics.title: POINTER MEANING
mnemonics.content: POINTER: POINTS TO INTERACT WITH NUMERIC ENTITIES REQUIRING MEMORY. Remember: A pointer INTERACTS with data by STORING the ADDRESS where data EXISTS in MEMORY.

mnemonics.title: ADDRESS AND DEREFERENCE
mnemonics.content: USE & TO GET ADDRESS, USE * TO GET VALUE. Think: ampersand (&) = 'at this location', asterisk (*) = 'the value at this location'.

mnemonics.title: POINTER DECLARATION
mnemonics.content: DECLARE FIRST, THEN ASTERISK. int *ptr means: ptr is of type 'pointer to int'. The * belongs to the variable, not the type.

mnemonics.title: CONST POINTER RULES
mnemonics.content: CONST NEAREST TO POINTER NAME CHANGES WHAT IS CONST. const int *p = const value through p (p can change). int *const p = p cannot change (value through p can change). const int *const p = neither can change.

acronyms.expansion: Memory Allocation - allocates specified bytes of heap memory
Contiguous Allocation - allocates and initializes memory to zero
Reallocation - changes size of previously allocated memory
Free Memory - releases dynamically allocated memory
No Object Reference - indicates pointer doesn't point to valid memory

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pointer as an ARROW pointing to a box in memory. The arrow contains the ADDRESS of the box, not the value inside. The * operator 'follows' the arrow to see what's inside.
Picture memory as a row of houses (bytes). An int pointer on a 32-bit system jumps 4 houses ahead when you add 1. A char pointer jumps only 1 house. The compiler handles the 'walking'.
Array name is like a signpost that ALWAYS points to the first house (element 0). You can walk from house to house using pointer arithmetic, but you CANNOT move the signpost.
NULL is like a 'No Address' sign - the pointer doesn't point anywhere valid. Dereferencing NULL is like trying to knock on a house that doesn't exist - program crashes!

analogies.analogy: A pointer is like a MAILBOX with an ADDRESS written on it. The variable is like the HOUSE at that address. The pointer (mailbox) doesn't contain the house, just tells where to find it.
If variable = HOUSE, pointer = ADDRESS on envelope, then dereferencing = actually OPENING the envelope to see what's inside (the house's contents).
Like a MAP (pointer to pointer) showing where to find another MAP (first pointer), which shows where to find the TREASURE (actual data).

Static memory is like living in a pre-built house (fixed size). Dynamic memory is like CONSTRUCTING your own house at runtime - you get exactly the size you need, but you must destroy it when done (free()).
